comments.title: Xian
comments.content: Hi JMG, in the context of your study of supernatural beings have you researched much about the Xian/Sennin? (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xian_(Taoism)). They are usually described as humans who have become immortal through spiritual cultivation. It's thought that the Arabic jinn may be derived from descriptions of the xian that came to the Middle East from China. Note that the story of Aladdin's lamp is supposed to take place in China. Do you draw any parallels between accounts of these beings and any other type of entity? The only comparable accounts I can recall are of involve Indian mystics. Do you think achieving such a state is within the scope of human potential or could these be a type of fae or other deceptive creature?

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content:
Yes, though I'm old enough that I'm still used to the Wade-Giles spelling, hsien. There's a great deal of similarity between the Chinese concept and the Western concept of the alchemical adept, who in some versions also has miraculous powers and is no longer subject to sickness and death -- not surprising, as there seems to have been a lot of cross-pollination between Chinese, Indian, and Western alchemy.

I suspect that the more colorful accounts of the hsien are to be taken symbolically rather than literally, but great longevity and unusual abilities do seem to be within the reach of certain intensive spiritual disciplines.

The jinn, though, are a different kind of critter. Jinn is the Arabic pronunciation of the Latin word genius, which meant a guardian spirit before it took on its modern sense of an unusually smart person.

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content:
Here's a different work regarding angels (from the same source quoted a few weeks ago):

https://wahiduddin.net/mv2/I/I_III_1.htm

The soul, which has passed through the angelic heavens in its descent to earth, comes next into the sphere of the Jinn or genius. This is the sphere of mind, and may be called the spiritual sphere, for it is mind and soul which make spirit. The souls who halt in this sphere, being attracted by its beauty, settle there; also the souls who have no power to go further into outer manifestation become the inhabitants of this sphere. [...] The jinn is an entity with a mind; but not a mind like that of man; a mind more pure, more clear, and illuminated by the light of intelligence. The mind of the jinn is deeper in perception and in conception, because it is empty, not filled with the thoughts and imaginations as is that of man.

[...]We have not adequate words to explain what the jinn is like, or what the world of the jinn is; but what little can be said that it is a world of music, art, poetry; a world of intelligence, cheerfulness and joy; a world of thought, imagination and sentiment; a world that a poet would long for and a musician would crave to dwell in. The life of the jinn is an ideal life for a thinker; a life which is free from all illness, pure from all bitterness of human nature, free to move about through space without any hindrance. This sphere is a most joyful place, where the sun of intelligence shines; where the trouble of birth and death is not so severe, life not so short as on the earth. If there is any paradise it is the world of the jinn. Hindus have called it Indra-Loka, and picture Gandharvas and Apsaras to be there; it is a paradise, of which every prophet has spoken to his followers in the way in which they could understand it.

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content: This reminds me of Kame Sennin (Master Roshi) character in Dragon Ball manga/anime series. Roshi is a very old master of martial arts and he has some superhuman abilities that are related his adeptness in using his vital energy. He is also said to have drank the "elixir of immortality". He must have been modeled after an archetypal xian/sennin being.

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content: Yeah, he's named as a sennin (仙人) so he's supposed to be a superhuman immortal, and Chinese legends often draw connections between sennin and martial arts masters. Note that the character for sen/xian 仙 is a combination of the kanji for person and mountain, so it's suggestive of ascetics living in the mountains. The manga series that really explores the sennin mythos is Houshin Engi, or "establishment of the gods," based on the classical Chinese novel of the same name. It has a cast full of sennin with distinct powers, weapons and accompanying magical creatures.

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content: Another related Japanese term is yamabushi, 山伏, "one who makes prostrations on the mountain" or "one who sleeps on the mountain" (translations vary). There's that very strong connection to mountain energy.

replies.title: Re: Xian
replies.content:
The Xian must also be related to the whole kung-fu stereotype of the old wise master living on a mountain in a monastery with a radical white beard & eyebrows and superhuman martial arts prowess. I always wondered about that, but this explains it.

I think Aladdin took place in Western China, near Central Asia.

As for djinn, they're a different kind of entity for sure. I read somewhere that they are like fey spirits attuned to the fire element, but I'm not certain.

--xcalibur/djs

comments.title: On lost things
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

This question might be stupid or impossible but I thought I would still ask. Many years ago I lost a piece of jewellery, a bracelet. The bracelet was very dear to me and had lots of sentimental value. As it normally works, I so diligently tried to keep it safe that I ended up losing it.

I was recently able to find an almost exact copy of the bracelet i lost. My question is, is there some way of consecrating or transferring the invested emotions to the new bracelet? I understand I cannot turn the new bracelet into the old but I was hoping that I could somehow designate it as the replacement.

replies.title: Re: On lost things
replies.content: It's not a stupid question at all. This is something that a simple ritual can do. Place a cloth on a table or other flat surface. Imagine the lost bracelet sitting on the cloth; make it as vivid as you can, and call up all the emotional associations connected with it. Feel those present in the imagined bracelet. Then place the new bracelet exactly in the same place as the one you have imagined, and sense all the feelings and memories of the old bracelet flowing into its substance. Say seven times, "What was lost is found, what was gone has returned, the circle is complete." Then put on the new bracelet and wear it for at least an hour, preferably more. That should do the trick.

replies.title: Re: On lost things
replies.content: Thanks so much, this is brilliant

replies.title: Re: On lost things
replies.content: Thank you for this. My husband lost his family-heirloom wedding ring and we’ve been considering having a replacement made using mine as a model. There’s much history in this set, so I’m happy to know of a way to kindle what was into what will be.

replies.title: Re: On lost things
replies.content: By all means use this same ritual for that -- it's generally applicable.

comments.title: Natural Magic
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
I have a natural magic question. Natural magic uses herbs, stones, and such. All these substances channel/store the energies of the planets. Let's say someone wants to work toward a sunny disposition and uses chamomile and St. John's Wort; pretty straightforward - Sun in Leo in the house of the rulership. However, some plants channel/store the energies of the planets in their fall/detriment. Let's take onion - Mars in Taurus (detriment). Mars who wants to be fast is highly uncomfortable in Taurus where everything is slow. Another example is leek - Jupiter in Gemini (also, detriment). Jupiter wants to expend and gets disoriented in Gemini where everything is pulled in many different directions. These are protective plants. I don't understand that. How can a plant be beneficial when it channels/stores energy that is ... er... detrimental?
Thank you for being here tonight,
Kirsten

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content:
Just to correct myself, not the house of rulership, the sign of rulership.
Kirsten

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: In traditional occult philosophy, the material world is the place where everything is muddled and nothing is clear-cut. Most of the time that's a disadvantage, but this is a case where it's not. Mars may be in his detriment in Taurus but he can also be stubborn and steadfast there, a good quality for a protective herb. Similarly, if Jupiter's in Mercury, in a muddled-up place like the material plane, he can expand through all the shifting directions Gemini offers him. Also, the material plane being muddled, a protective power can be found in something you wouldn't expect to be protective!

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content:
Thank you for your reply, JMG. I get it now, but would like to clarify. You say, "...the material world is the place where everything is muddled and nothing is clear-cut. Most of the time that's a disadvantage". Would you please give some examples of muddled energies of the material world and corresponding disadvantages? I'd like to understand the whole continuum from a usual disadvantage to the rare situations of an advantage in unexpected places (as in the protective properties of leeks and onions).
Kirsten

replies.title: Re: Natural Magic
replies.content: That's a subject for a post, not a brief answer! The very short form is that natural magic is less powerful than, say, astrological magic, because the planetary influences in an herb are never unmixed -- there's always some of every planet and every other influence even when one is predominant.

comments.title: Recognition of other people in Past lives
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I remember reading something you wrote where you mention that you and your late wife were married in previous lives. This brings up two questions I hope you can answer:

1) When you remember a past life, how do you know which person is "you"
2) When you see someone in a past life and then see them in this life, how do you recognize these two bodies as the same person?

Just curious--thanks!

replies.title: Re: Recognition of other people in Past lives
replies.content:
1) Think of your own childhood memories. If you're like most people, you have no question who "you" is in those memories -- they're first-person recollections, from the viewpoint you had at the time, seen through your eyes and heard through your ears. Exactly the same thing is true of past life memories. I don't remember things as though I was watching a movie -- they're like any other memory.

2) That, on the other hand, can be difficult. Sara and I figured things out fairly quickly, once we both had past life memories coming through, because we remembered some of the same things -- the fatal car crash that ended both our last lives, for example, and certain other events that were important. Mannerisms, habits, and attitudes can also offer some guidance, since those tend to remain from life to life.

replies.title: Re: Recognition of other people in Past lives
replies.content:
Can confirm, past life memories are personal & subjective, a mix of thoughts, words, visuals, feelings, etc. just like memories in this life. The main difference I notice is that they tend to bubble up into your awareness, they feel different than imagination, and you may not realize what it is right away. As to the second part, I can't really comment.

--xcalibur/djs

comments.title: High Magic
comments.content:
Good Evening, Mr. Greer,

I am doing a delve into High Magic, and was wondering if you might describe or clarify what the Holy Guardian Angel is. I take it to be the higher self, but the term seems to imply that it is something already developed, and which can guide us?

Also, would pathworking such as you’ve written about or like the GD include work with the HGA?

Is it a Cabalistic concept? Or a medieval Christian invention?

Thank you, kindly!

E. S.

replies.title: Re: High Magic
replies.content:
1) There's a lot of disagreement about this in the occult scene. As I see it, the higher self is one thing and the Holy Guardian Angel is quite another. The higher self is your individuality, the true self that you really are and have always been, which is temporarily veiled by the personality you've taken on for this incarnation. The guardian angel or, as the older Pagan traditions call it, the guardian genius is a spiritual being who has been guiding and protecting you since the beginning of your journey through incarnations.

2) Not really. You can use pathworkings for that purpose but that's not a particularly effective method for that.

3) Neither one. Religious and spiritual traditions from around the world and throughout time have had this same concept. The idea of the guardian genius, the protecting and guiding spirit, was common in Pagan times long before Christianity or the Cabala were thought of; Roman Pagans would burn incense and pour a libation of wine on their birthday to their guardian spirit to thank it for its help over the year just past and ask it for help in the year to come.

replies.title: Re: High Magic
replies.content: In what exactly consist the protection of the Higher Guardian Angel, since people nevertheless make all kinds of mistakes?

replies.title: Re: High Magic
replies.content: The HGA can't override your free will, and so you can make whatever blunders you want. All it can do is offer you advice, and you have to be willing to listen. Your conscience is usually its voice; so is that awkward sense that tells you that you're about to do something very, very stupid. If you attend to those, and put work into clearing your mind in meditation, you can start hearing it more clearly.

replies.title: Re: High Magic
replies.content:
I would like to take up the practice you mention in point 3, and make an offering of incense and a libation to my guardian iuno on my birthday.

That got me thinking of where I would pour out the libation. Did people who were not members of the Imperial family have home shrines to their genius/iuno? Would it be with the shrine to the Lares and Penates? (Assuming they were rich enough to have a private house.) Did they go somewhere else to burn the incense and pour out the libation? To the Temple of Juno, perhaps? Is there an image or symbol involved as a focus? Does it involve an object that was given to one as a child?

Would this be an indoor or outdoor ceremony, or does it matter?

replies.title: Re: High Magic
replies.content: It was normally done indoors at the household shrine to the lares and penates. The libation, as usual in indoors ceremonies, was poured into a wide earthenware bowl set aside for that purpose, and later taken and poured on the earth. No, there was no image, symbol, or object. On the other hand, the Orphic Hymn to the genius, no. 72 in the usual order, might be recited by those who had some Greek scholarship (as most educated Romans did).

comments.title: What Do We Want?
comments.content:
1) I think this is a very important topic that doesn’t get talked about enough and was touched on in last week’s Magic Monday. Last week’s reply:

“It can take any amount of time -- whole lives, for example -- to figure out that "what you want" is usually a self-contradictory mess of automatic cravings picked up from family, friends, and mass media, which will make a mess of your life rather than bringing you the happiness you think they'll provide. That's when the door opens and the real work begins.”

I loved any stories about magic/mystery/secret knowledge/powers at age 11. Then I turned 13 and that was all swept away in every kind of “self-contradictory mess of automatic cravings” combined with addictions galore. This led to terrible life decisions and when things weren’t turning out very well I went into research mode to figure out what the hell am I involved in here..like, what is all this?? Started externally with how our current system really works, which you have broken down quite masterfully. Many years later caught a well known occultist on a podcast talking about magic and that got me back on the path so to speak, albeit slowly, having to shake off the Catholic upbringing. The crazy thing is in doing the workings to get the high paying job or the dream girl, when they appeared I couldn’t handle them lol. That job means cold calling people for 8 hours a day, developing a client list and becoming known as Mr. So-and-so in the community. That blond beauty at the gym that came my way landing 2 feet from me in a quiet empty gym on a Sunday? didn’t have the chops to tackle that one! Your great secret of magic was understood after a few of these incidents.

That has brought me back to the question “what do you want?”. That has never been really clear to me which is why I got derailed so easily in the first place! You wrote “that's when the door opens and the real work begins”. I am at that point. The process of de-hypnotization has become much more noticeable. Can you speak on ways people can start working this out? It reminds me of questions like “what would you do if you had infinite money?” or “what would you do if you were invisible”? You may even cut down on the Magic Monday repeat questions on grimoires and pentacles to get stuff.

2) Do you think there is value in pointing the direction for people trying to bully the cosmos into getting what they want? Something like “The fool that persists in his folly becomes wise”. Would this speed up the learning curve? I can say I was never one not to do something because somebody said it was a bad idea, but it was apparent to me early on with magic this can be catastrophic.

I don’t usually write longer questions, but your quoted words at the top, to me, are the crux of so many issues for people.

Thank you for Magic Monday

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?
replies.content:
1) I wish I had a straightforward answer to this! It took me a long time and a lot of frustrations to realize that what I wanted and what I thought I wanted weren't always the same thing -- and I was lucky, because some of the things I thought I wanted (a career as a writer, a happy marriage) were things I did in fact want, and got, and loved. I have immense sympathy for those who aren't so fortunate, and get the things they think they wanted, and end up utterly miserable as a result. All I can say is that some people, if they're introduced to the concept you're discussing, seem to be able to figure it out.

2) No, not at all. For many people, perhaps most, that way lies total ruin, and a cascade of nasty karma that may take many lives to unravel. I've become increaasingly convinced that the older occult tradition, which downplayed magic in favor of meditation and psychic development, had the better idea all along.

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?
replies.content:
What our host said (in answer to your #2 question)! Ever since I was a 'teen, I've always had a very strong sense that magic is the magician's very last resort, to be used rarely and with great wariness.

That said, there are situations when only magic will do. And even in such a situation, there are sometimes great costs for the magician. Magic isn't a all-purpose fix-it.

Likewise, I came to the idea that magic as such doesn't teach much wisdom, it's rather occult philosophy and practice which teaches it. That said, I find magic to be a valid spiritual practice.

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?
replies.content:
Its nice to hear it wasn't a straight forward process for you too. My slow journey into magic has helped me ditch so many bad habits if only through having a framework to understand  a larger reality and a theory of "how things work" that I didnt find the current form of traditional religions made clear in a practical way. The experimental nature of magic is definitely attractive for pushing oneself into unfamiliar territory. The examples of being led to the doorstep of what I thought I wanted taught me so much about my current state and the changes in myself and my expectations I would have to make. Not sure about magic as a last resort...I think small magic interwoven in everyday life and big magic less often...One thing I will say is many things in life can be changed but picking the wrong line of work is one of the worst things. A career oriented astrological reading combined with some psychological testing if someone isn't sure of what they're 'cut out for' could help avoiding the weeds.

It sounds like I'm heading in the right direction just started a little late. Is the Bardon path closer to the older occult tradition?

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?
replies.content: Bardon's work is classic central European occultism, which differs in important ways from the Anglo-American tradition; it falls somewhere in between the classic occultism I mentioned and the magic-centered approach so common these days. That said, it's a good robust system and has excellent results so long as you do each step as thoroughly as Bardon advises. Failures in Bardon's system happen when people pick and choose, or scant the practices they don't like (such as the black and white mirrors) and focus on the practices they do like.

replies.title: Re: What Do We Want?
replies.content:
The de-hypnotization process isn't often aided by questions like “what would you do if you had infinite money?” or “what would you do if you were invisible?”, since society mostly utilizes those in order to get us more profoundly disoriented and entangled. Instead, attempting to ponder how we wound up so confused and uncomfortably distanced from our deeper Self tends to be far more liberating. Questions like "who would I be if I weren't so burdened by doubts and insecurities?" or "how would I experience the world if I felt naturally connected to every energy in it?" are much more likely to help in the unfolding of our lives' purpose, which is another way of saying "what we truly want" or "why we came into incarnation".

When the door opens and the real work begins of distinguishing between the self-contradictory mess of what we think we want and why we're actually here, every energy in the universe is available to help us in that complex work. Every curse, addiction, and demonic obsession we struggle to cast off can become our next teacher on the path. Every craving, missed opportunity, and attempt to bully can help extricate us from the webs of illusion we've gotten caught up in.

When that real work begins, pondering "how would I experience myself if I trusted that all of my incongruous and unseemly intuitions and instincts were simply attempts by my vastly larger Self to reveal its unplumbed depths to my conscious awareness?" can act as a wonderful natural solvent for the myriad burdens we have wrestled over for so many lives. Or, we can simply wonder at the magnificent nature of divinity, which will amount to the very same contemplation, eventually.

— Christophe

comments.title: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
comments.content:
Hey JMG,
A topic which always got me curious was Astral phenomena, so I decided to do some research of my own. Atkinson seemed like as good a place as any to start, so I read (not in the proper meditative way, just out of curiosity) some of Atkinson's "Swami Panchadasi" works. My questions are:

1. Atkinson colorfully describes the Astral plane. How much of that is accurate and how much are there embellishments or omissions in his account? (He does, of course, mention omitting a lot. How much is that "a lot"?) Of course I'm not asking for a proper description, just how trustworthy for a novice are these descriptions (I should mention I don't intend to muck about in that plane any time soon).

2. This may be way above my pay grade, but is it truly possible to consult the Akashic records? Are they a real thing? Looking at the past directly would be an amazing thing, though I expect if it is at all possible it takes a proper occult master to do so. If they are real, have you seen them yourself or know of anyone who has done so?

3. I've noticed the colors of the elements in the SoP seem to be somewhat related to the color of the auras as Atkinson describes them. Is that coincidence, i.e. the colors of the symbols have another origin, or is there something to this idea? I noticed that for example, yellow, the color of intellectual efforts in the aura, is the color of air, which so far as I gather the element more closely related to intellectual pursuits.

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content:
1) It's one person's experience of certain parts of the astral plane: accurate enough as far as that goes, but it's as though somebody from overseas took a walk through a neighborhood in a small town in Delaware and described it in a letter to friends back home saying, "This is what America is like." If you happen to end up in Florida, Colorado, or Alaska, you'll see very different things!

2) Nope. The theory that the images in the akasha are accurate records of the past was very widely held back in Atkinson's time, and occultists set out to test it. It turns out that it's just like remote viewing: a few amazing successes in a much vaster amount of utter nonsense.

3) It's not a coincidence. Lots of people in the early 20th century learned to see auras, and what they saw helped shape color symbolism in occult teachings.

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content:
Thank you for the response. I figured the Akashic Records sounded like a little too good to be true.

I suspected there was a connection with the colors; it's interesting to see these details show up and to be able to notice them and draw these potential connections.

Two further, brief, questions: I'm very interested in seeing auras. Is Atkinson a good enough source to follow, or do you have any specific recommendations? And am I getting in over my head and should leave this business for later, or is there no big danger in being able to see auras?

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content: Atkinson's a good source, and this used to be something a lot of relative beginners did. Just be aware you may see things you don't want to see -- if you can read auras, for example, you can fairly often see someone's actual feelings, and that can involve unpleasant discoveries.

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content: I'd much rather an unpleasant truth than a comfortable illusion; or that's what I'm saying to myself anyway. I'd just like to see the auras, it seems cool. Thank you very much as always for the answers.

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content: I was wondering if I should say something. The universe has a very bad habit of taking that kind of bravado as a challenge, and showing you things you really, truly don't want to see. That said, yes, seeing auras is cool, and it also has its advantages.

replies.title: Re: William Atkinson's Swami Panchadasi Works
replies.content:
Just a small footnote:

Although the evidence is not entirely conclusive, "Swami Panchdasi" and "Swami Bhakta Vishita" are probably not pseudonyms of William Walker Atkinson, but of his associate Claude Alexander Conlin (1880-1954). Conlin was more famous as a stage magician, and there is a good biography of him as such by David Charvet, Alexander, the Man Who Knows (2nd revised edition, 2007), published at Pasadena, CA, by Mike Caveney's Magic Words. He's the man who founded the Crystal Silence League, revived by cat yronwode a decade or three ago.

comments.title: astral travel in sleep?
comments.content:
I'm wondering, is it possible to astral travel in your sleep? I ask this because I've had a series of "dreams" which are of a different kind than usual. You said you don't analyze dreams, but this may be different, since these experiences, all similar, are very different from the usual vivid jumble of dreaming. I'll delve into them here in hopes of gaining insights on this, and the entities I encountered along the way. Generally speaking, my dream-body is more corporeal than usual, I'm in a version of my house (usually from years ago) and it's all quite detailed and realistic, so much so that I can see lint/dust/etc. Yet there's a sense of strangeness about it, as if it's a hologram; it's also silent, and I can't see out any windows.

On a few different occasions, I encountered an entity that didn't appear before me, rather it levitated me and caused me to float around against my will. I would resist, and then I'd move wildly and wake up; another time it leaned me close to a mirror, where I saw a younger version of myself, then I woke up.

More recently, I encountered a couple of unique entities. One was like a very young girl, babbling to herself in my hallway, I was worried, and sealed the door shut against her, but nothing more happened. Shortly after, I was in another room, there was another entity like an older fat male, standing rather close and breathing heavily, but said nothing, and nothing else happened. Even though they took on a human aspect, I could sense that they weren't really human.

Another time there were no entities, but I closed one dream-eye and then the other, realized the blind-spot from my medical condition wasn't there, and knew that I was dreaming. I then felt my body laying in bed some distance away, drifted towards it, melded back in, then woke up. That experience was a bit more pleasant, and I'm almost positive I was projecting out of my physical form somehow?

Then there was the "ghost". It was solid, not translucent, it was like if you made a ghost out of white bedsheets, bunching/knotting them to make the head, letting the rest drape down. This was a truly hostile entity. It hovered by the foot of my bed, staring at me, I stared back with difficulty, it was like putting two like magnets together. I myself wasn't afraid, but it radiated fear into me as a psychic attack. I resisted the fear, and its attempts to force me to look away, I exerted my will and stared back in defiance, and after a time I woke up, having successfully resisted the "ghost". I remember gazing warily at that spot by the foot of my bed, but of course there was nothing there, not on the material plane...

Having shared the darkest one, I'll finish with the lightest one. This was a little different than the others, it was in another house (but one that's important), it was similar but didn't feel as uncanny. In this one, I encountered a relative of mine who is deceased, who seemed healthier and better off than ever. We exchanged words, and as I left and went to the car in the driveway, I woke up (I could see the outside and hear just fine this time). That was the most pleasant of them, although it was bittersweet. I wonder if that was a genuine contact with my relative's spirit?

To conclude, I'd like to know what you make of these experiences, the entities I encountered, whether I was astral traveling or doing something else on another plane, and any other relevant advice. As always, you don't have to explain all points, I'm just trying to ask the right stuff. eta: pardon the lengthy post, I wanted to provide plenty of detail!

--xcalibur/djs

replies.title: Re: astral travel in sleep?
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: astral travel in sleep?
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Dreams are perceptions of the astral plane. Most dreams are not much more than astral static, but now and again you drift far enough out of your physical body to see things clearly. Some of the old occult schools used to teach dreamwork as the easiest way to access the astral plane.

A dream journal in which you write down every dream you remember as soon as you awaken is essential for this kind of work. Many people find that if they do this every morning, it will lead to lucid dreaming, and that opens the door to conscious astral travel in sleep.

replies.title: Re: astral travel in sleep?
replies.content:
1) It really depends on personal factors. A year is a good start, certainly.

2) Unfortunately the sources I know of are private correspondence courses for magical orders, but there's a substantial literature on lucid dreaming, which you might look into. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: astral travel in sleep?
replies.content:
I've recommended this before, but my two go-to books are Creative Dreaming by Patricia Garfield and Dreams, A Portal to the Source by Eric C. Whitmont and Sylvia Brinton Perera, though I also found Jordan Peterson's work on archetypes and his Jung-inspired discussion of dreams helpful (if you want a book rather than podcasts/videos, his Maps of Meaning will likely do you more good than his more recent ones, though it's more of a slog).

Creative Dreaming is a full "how to" on cultivating memory of dreams, working toward lucid dreaming, and the kind of stuff you might hope to get out of such work. Dreams is a Jungian guide to dream interpretation, and Maps of Meaning is Peterson giving a way of thinking about stories and archetypes that is useful because of its flexibility, but it gets that way by being rather high-level (for example, it'll give great insight into what a figure in a dream or story being female might signify, but won't have much to say on why it's your sister vs your cousin vs your neighbor, or what have you).

I haven't done dreamwork in a while, but I got some useful insights when I did.

Hope these help, and good luck!
Jeff

comments.title: NTHE, Progress
comments.content:
Dear John,
I am wondering your thoughts about near-term human extinction (NTHE). It seems to have become almost an unconscious pall hanging over and unsettling a traumatized and collapse-weary humanity. With regard to the collapse of "advanced" industrialized (homo colossus) civilization I recall that the late Michael Dowd argued that "this time is different", i.e. that the unprecedented GLOBAL climate catastrophe (and impending doom and destruction) adds a whole new ingredient/dimension to the mix of the "standard" historical collapse of societies over millennia that you have proffered. How would you respond to this? Also to Guy McPherson's projection of the extinction of all life on earth by 2030 (and possibly of all human life by 2026), accelerated even more once the looming/overdue "blue ocean event" or end of Arctic/Antarctic sea ice, alongside the stripping away of atmospheric ozone and melting down of the world's 400+ nuclear power plants and thousands of "spent" nuclear fuel rods, occurs?

Admittedly this has long been a depressing and horrifying thought for my idealist/romantic self to digest and accept. The other part of my earnest question is, can you, drawing upon your vast spiritual background and knowledge of esoterica, reframe this to offer an even "longer view" where on the other side of collapse something transformative and better will emerge from the rubble? Or, supposing NTHE does occur, given the premise of the soul/spirit/Monad's eternal nature and constitution, do you believe that millions of years hence through the material-evolutionary process human souls would return and begin to incarnate on earth (or elsewhere) once again?

Finally, relatedly, do you believe "ultimately" in the telos of evolutionary progress (not referring here to the shorter-term "myth of [material/physical] progress" that you have convincingly debunked)? I concur that what we call "progress" is cyclical rather than linear. What will/would such "ultimate" progress entail metaphysically for you? Might not it be analagous on macro levels of planet/solar system/galaxy/universe etc to the evolution of the individual spirit/Monad through ascending kingdoms of nature and hierarchies over many aeons (as below, so above)? Tolstoy has a quote, "The only progress that truly matters is spiritual progress." Thank you for doing this work.

PS On a lighter note I once heard you mention you wish Monty Python had pursued or tackled some comedy about philosophy or philosophers. They did indeed write one song on the topic. Are you familiar with "The Drunken Philosophers' Song" (linked below)? Incidentally, a side note that Eric Idle had mentioned he got the line backwards when chiding the live audience during the song's performance: he meant to say "The ADULTS are all on drugs and the KIDS are all on roller skates"! They laughed at it anyway!

PPS Have you read a lot of Alasdair Macintyre? I heard you reference his landmark "After Virtue", which I find is an amazing and original inquiry into moral history/philosophy...I came across that and Macintyre's work in general when studying philosophy at Villanova.

https://youtu.be/6b7r5jIEe9s?si=_vcA2KZniqn33k-x

Anthony Farina
Pittsburgh PA

replies.title: Re: NTHE, Progress
replies.content:
1) Yes, I've heard some of the chatter about imminent human extinction. It's simply the latest rehash of standard religious apocalyptic rhetoric, decked out in secular drag. I see it as the Boomer generation's collective freakout in response to its own impending death from old age -- "It can't just be me that's dying, everybody's got to die!" (I say this, by the way, as a Boomer; if I can't t-t-t-talk about my generation, who can?) It's always possible to come up with wildly overinflated worst case scenarios and use those to justify the claim that we're all going to die, but if you take a little while to read older works along these lines, you'll find that these same claims have been made over and over again for decades, and we've already passed by quite a number of dates by which everyone was supposed to be dead. I wrote about that back in 2013:

https://thearchdruidreport-archive.200605.xyz/2013/05/the-pleasures-of-extinction.html

2) Stop right there. Why should what rises out of the rubble be "transformative and better"? What possible reason do we have for supposing that this is the case? To my mind this is just one more dreary serving of the mythology of progress. The sooner we outgrow the delusion that the universe is obligated to hand us a better world sometime in the future, the better.

3) Nope. I don't believe in that version of the progressivist myth either. As I see it, spiritual development is always an individual matter, and it doesn't lead us all in a straight line toward some single goal. Each soul has its own unique potentials, and while it makes sense for us to follow established spiritual paths now -- we're still quite early in our unfolding, you know -- in due time each of us will become something unique, and follow a path no other can follow.

4) I remember it well!

5) I haven't read as much of him as I'd like; here in Rhode Island the university libraries won't let hoi polloi check out books, as academic libraries do in most other places, and so I don't have easy access to a lot of recent scholarly publications.

If so does not this accumulation of individual Monadic/spiritual progress constitute or lead to some kind of collective progress or communal higher state of consciousness outside/beyond this temporal material speck? That is, wouldn't collective progress in the very long view mirror and result from individual progress? replies.title: Re: NTHE, Progress
replies.content:
It's more complex than that. Souls descend into matter in swarms; in the system I follow, ours is the fourth such swarm, and we haven't all descended at once -- the first of us started around 600 million years ago, when multicellular life first appeared. (For perspective, the first swarm started its journey something like 10 billion years ago, when the dust cloud that became the solar system first formed.) There are three more swarms still to come during the lifespan of this solar system. Each swarm eventually becomes an angelic order -- the first three swarms are now the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind, and when we complete our journey we hall be the Lords of Freedom. Each swarm creates patterns and possibilities that are taken up by the later swarms; the Lords of Flame are vastly beyond us, but when we finish our evolution we will have capacities and gifts they cannot yet imagine, and the swarms that come after us will surpass us in the same way.

Once the solar system finishes its life cycle, souls that have passed through evolution here will go on to other destinies depending on their character and capacities. At the upper end of the whole process, perfected souls will merge back into the Absolute from which they came. Meanwhile new souls will be coming into being the whole time. That's why there's no progress as such. For every soul that rises above the human level there's another soul that has just achieved the human level, and drags the average back down closer to the animal level. Humanity is a transitional stage; it is what it is for good and sufficient reason, and those who have learned its lessons go on to other things while new souls come into it like children filing into a classroom.

replies.title: Re: NTHE, Progress
replies.content: Not the OP, but in this system what is the point of the journey? If we travel from the absolute back to the absolute, why take such a journey?

replies.title: Re: NTHE, Progress
replies.content: We can speculate about that, but neither you nor I nor any human being can know. Maybe Sri Aurobindo was right and the cosmos really is an eternal child playing an eternal game in an eternal garden.

replies.title: Re: NTHE, Progress
replies.content:
There is a wide gulf between civilizational collapse and human extinction. The former has happened several times, the latter only happened to other human species in distant prehistory, largely as a result of homo sapiens taking over the world. Since our species is not being replaced by another, extinction is nothing to worry about. Even after the severity of the Bronze Age collapse, civilization rose again after a centuries-long Dark Age, creating new societies, and new writing systems as the old were forgotten (in the case of Linear A/B), relegated to priests/scholars (cuneiform), or gradually declined (hieroglyphics). Given our technology, and our presence around the world, it would be quite difficult for an extinction event to take us out, even nuclear war would only be sufficient to topple civilization IMO.

So yes, anything you hear about human extinction is likely just boomer apocalyptic rhetoric, as stated by JMG.

--xcalibur/djs

comments.content:
a) In your essay on a Magical Education, you suggest learning a language for the practical purpose of gaining access to serious occult work which no one in the English world has access to. I wonder if there's more to it though. I'm learning Arabic, and although I'm currently only at the point where I can talk to people about basic things like the weather, watch TV news and make some sense of it if I rewind and re-watch it several times (and usually in large part due to piecing context together from the images and my general knowledge of world affairs), and read children's books with some effort. I am nowhere near being able to tackle anything remotely complicated in the language, but I am already noticing it's helping certain aspects of my occult practices. Arabic is completely different from English; it's written in a different alphabet that's read right to left, writing is always cursive, and the language is usually written with limited vowel markings; there are sounds English lacks entirely (such as the "emphatic" consonants), distinctions English doesn't make (such as between long and short vowels), and standard Arabic merges sounds English considers separate (such as "p" and "b"); there is no verb meaning "to be" in the present tense; and the list of major differences goes on.

What I'm noticing is that I'm now working with language as a symbolic system, and not a reality; and the practice I'm getting with shifting between two radically different symbolic systems at will is making it easier for me to do this in my occult work. The differences in the symbolic systems between the elements, the planets, and the spheres, to provide one example, has begun to become a lot easier to work with; and then there's the far from minor point that the Arabic World is radically different from ours, and engaging with their world in an unsanitized fashion is forcing me to notice my own internalized cultural biases in ways that I never did before, which is a major boost to the set of skills needed to be an occultist; as well as a weird general improvement in certain practices that were heavily utilized in the Arab world, such as geomancy.

It's subtle, and I think the effect is stronger than with, for example, French, because Arabic is a very different language from English, but the effort I've put into learning it has already been a huge boost to my occult practices. You've said before that you can read at a minimum English, French, and Latin; and I think you said you've picked up some Portuguese and German as well, so you have first hand experience with learning another language as an occultist, and might have seen things like I'm noticing with Arabic. Were you thinking of any of these factors when you discussed learning another language in A Magical Education?

b) I'm thinking of starting a blog. I settled on starting it on a Wednesday during the hour of Mercury, with the Moon waxing in Virgo or Gemini. I found a time with these factors, with the added benefit of having Mercury well dignified in Gemini and on the ascendant; with the Sun also in Gemini. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't think there is historical evidence that religions in general have a particular lifespan. We should narrow this question to world religions, because religions usually start in a particular location or among a specific ethnic group; if the religion does not spread, it is vulnerable to the fortunes of the group that practices it.

Among world religions, Hinduism and Buddhism are flourishing. Buddhism is five hundred years older than Christianity, Hinduism about twice that. Judaism in some form has been practiced for about three thousand years and is holding its own. Zoroastrianism is about the same age as Buddhism, but the population that practices it is concentrated in one part of the world, which makes it vulnerable. Shinto is very old and doing all right, but is only now beginning to spread outside its homeland. Mithraism died out because its base was in the Roman Army, and it was suppressed when Christianity became the state religion of Rome.

Long-lived religions change with the times. Judaism has been rebooted repeatedly. When the circumstances in which the religion arose change, whether it be a change in government, economic system, or location, the religion is more likely to survive by creating ways of practice and teaching that are portable and adaptable to loss or gain of resources from the surrounding society.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There are various categories of souls who have completed their work as human beings but still involve themselves with the human world. Saints are one class, adepts another, and where the line should be drawn between them is a tough call -- St. Cyprian, for example, the patron saint of magic, arguably falls into both categories.

1a) Saints were human. Deities -- well, it depends on what you mean by that remarkably flexible category. In Japan, some kami were human beings and others were not, while in classical Greece those beings who were human but have transcended human status were heroes, not gods -- the gods were of a different and higher class, (And of course there were demigods, who were the offspring of a god or goddess and a human being.)

2) It's not something I've looked into, since I'm not Christian or Muslim.

replies.title: Re: Pre Beginner
replies.content:
You would want to consider taking up a course of training in occultism. All these preliminary practices were given out, lesson by lesson, before magic as such ever entered the picture, and they were combined with a good general survey of occult teachings.

There are, broadly speaking, three ways to get that these days. You can sign up for one of the surviving occult orders and take their correspondence course; you can find a course from a defunct order and practice it; or you can get a book, or a series of books, that provides the same experience. Any of these is a valid approach; all of them will work -- if you work with them -- and none of them will do any good otherwise.

1) Among the surviving orders that still offer the traditional training are the Builders of the Adytum at https://www.bota.org/, the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis at https://soul.org/ , the Brotherhood of Light at https://www.light.org/ , and the Societas Rosicruciana in America at https://sria.org/

2) The IAPSOP lessons archive is a very mixed bag but it has quite a few complete courses; it's at http://iapsop.com/lessons/ .

3) William Walker Atkinson's books are good sources here; his Arcane Teaching, Arcane Formulas, and Vril are classics. My series of books on the Golden Section Fellowship, beginning with The Way of the Golden Section, is a modern attempt to provide the same kind of training.

comments.title: Scotland
comments.content:
JMG

I recently visited Scotland on holiday and did all the usual tourist stuff.

One of the historic tourist walks around Edinburgh - with some fun “spooky” stuff - pointed out that Scotland executed - on a per capita basis - by FAR the most number of witches of anywhere in Europe in the 15-17th centuries. Larger countries executed more, but Scotland was far ahead per capita.

Do you know any reason why that kind of mania was so bad in Scotland? This would mostly have been when it was still an independent kingdom, before the United Kingdom was a thing.

Also, presumably some small minority of the accused witches were actually real witches practicing black magic and trying to hurt others? For example, I forget the name of the woman, but they told us the story of one of the most famous witches - who had 20+ accusations of witchcraft against her, as opposed to the typical 1-2 - and the kind of thing she was accused of “causing a woman she disliked to fall off a horse and die” sounds like the kind of thing magic might actually be capable of doing?

I also visited Loch Ness and was thinking of the Nessie chapter in your book Monsters. I had no idea Loch Ness alone contains more water by volume than all other lakes and rivers in the United Kingdom *combined* (it is not the deepest or the longest loch, but it is very deep - 500-600 feet AND very long, and the combination means it has a huge volume of water), and there are lots of unidentified marine species in the depths of it hundreds of feet down even if Nessie has never been identified - there are frequently sonar contacts of very large fish/loch life - even my cruise had a sonar hit of something 2 metres long and I think the largest unidentified sonar contact in the last 6 months was something *5* metres long and 100 metres down.

I don’t know about Nessie, but the Loch has a lot that is unexplained about it even today. Stunningly beautiful and peaceful too.

Scotland was beautiful, especially the Highlands and noticeably more deserted and less populated than anywhere in England - not surprising since the whole of Scotland has barely more than the half the population of London or New York. Edinburgh only has half a million and Inverness (the capital of the Highlands since all the major Scottish cities are in the lowlands) only has 50,000 people!)

replies.title: Re: Scotland
replies.content:
Scotland's history in general is unbelievably bloody, and that's left deep marks on the culture. (I forget which Irish band used to say, "And now we're going to sing a song about violence, treachery, and murder -- that is, a Scottish love ballad." It always got a laugh but they weren't wrong.) It's where my paternal ancestors came from, but that doesn't change the fact that its a bleak, desolate, impoverished land that for centuries was constantly being raided and pillaged by its neighbors to the south, but also had constant internal struggles of astonishing savagery -- read any detailed account of Scottish politics back before the Act of Union and you'll see what I mean. So it's not surprising that when they got to work hunting witches they did it with unusual vigor. Mind you, it's quite possible they had more than their share of nasty magic as well!

As for Loch Ness, granted. Those deep glacial lakes have a lot of mysteries in them.

comments.title: Consecration of Talismans
comments.content:
JMG,

Hope you are well. I have a pendant that I would like to consecrate and wear. Can you point me in the right direction on how to do this? Any insights would be very helpful.

Thank you,

Scott B

replies.title: Re: Consecration of Talismans
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with basics. A talisman is a magical device that is charged and consecrated with a specific intention, and that has a carefully chosen shape, design, etc. to symbolize that intention. It's not usually worn -- and it must be virgin, i.e., not previously worn or used for any purpose, so it can take and hold the charge you give it. If your pendant has some specific symbolism you want to use as an anchor for a specific intention, and it's brand new, that's one thing, and you might be able to make a talisman from it. Otherwise, nope.

That leads to the next question: what kind of magic do you know how to do? Consecrating and charging talismans isn't something beginners can do effectively, any more than you could do a decent riff if you're just starting to learn the guitar.

comments.title: Dark Enchantment Article and Question on Adapting Evolved Traditions
comments.content:
Good Evening,

If you live in the US, I hope you've settled up to your satisfaction with our government, and can move on to enjoying some fine spring weather.

To Share: A counter to the spreading "re-paganizing" claim some Christians have been making that isn't exactly a defense of the old ways, but at least doesn't conflate them with nihilistic modernity, and touches on the idea of "re-enchantment" along the way: https://www.firstthings.com/article/2024/05/dark-enchantment

To Ask: I've long respected and admired things that are evolved/emergent over those that are consciously designed, and all the more so as the cracks have begun to show in the Faustian managerial project you so effectively skewered a few weeks back on the main blog. As such, I've tried to approach my spiritual practice with a respect for traditions, empirically-arrived-at approaches, and so forth over "systems" that are just a little too rational and coherent. Worshiping Gods who have not been widely worshiped in a thousand years, though, sometimes I find a need to invent - something doesn't exist that does what I'm looking for, or I see something another tradition does that I wish I had in my life, and so forth.

So anyway, the actual question: do you, JMG, or anyone else in the commentariat, have any tips for how to borrow as many of the strengths of the long-established, evolved practices or traditions when you find it necessary to come up with something new?

In case that's too abstract, here's a concrete example that I'm working on: I've been at my Heathen Rosary project for awhile now, and it's whole conception is "borrow as much of the evolved practice and 'what works' from the Catholic Rosary as I can while crafting something that fits in a heathen spiritual worldview." I've tried to identify, as much best I can, what seems more "structural" and what more "accidental," in order to keep more of the former and be willing to lose the latter, but of course there's plenty of room for error there. Mostly I've supplemented plain, old study with prayer, meditation, and divination, but any other approaches, tips, or the like would be most welcome.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Dark Enchantment Article and Question on Adapting Evolved Traditions
replies.content: Thanks for the article. As for your question, so long as you're consciously borrowing things, you're in the realm of conscious design. You need to reach into the realms of dream and fantasy in whatever way works for you -- omens, divination, active imagination, you name it -- to get past that.

replies.title: Re: Dark Enchantment Article and Question on Adapting Evolved Traditions
replies.content:
Of course! Lyons caused quite a stir about a year ago with his "China Convergence" article, which I might have shared on a recent open post, and is about the underlying managerial, technocratic rationale common to China's and the West's approaches, but that's not very "Magic Monday."

And hmm, thank you, I will have incorporate more "fantastic" ways of drawing on inspiration than the divination and meditation I'm doing now. It strikes me especially that I might do more to take sources of borrowing as the jumping off point for such imaginative work and see where that takes me, where to date I've mostly focused on the "target" in those ways.

comments.content: Hi JMG. What in occult terms determine someone's romantic 'type'?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's complex, because people are complex. Each of your bodies -- material, etheric, astral, and mental -- is primed to relate to certain other people via polarity: on the material plane that expresses itself as reproduction, on the etheric plane as sexual desire, on the astral plane as romantic attachment, and on the mental plane as unconditional love. Any given person may trigger none, one, more than one, or all of these, but of course "all of these" is the rarest of all the options. Most of us have encountered people who fire our gonads but don't touch our heart, and quite a few of us have encountered people who do the reverse. The more levels connect up, the more someone is your "type" -- and awkwardly, it doesn't always go both ways; a certain person can stir up all four of your bodies and but have no response to you on any. Thus the awkwardness of human relationships!
